lines.text: SEGMENT 18 — Closing: what to remember
We have covered a lot.
Let me close by giving you the things I most want you to remember.
One.
Pharmacogenomics is the genetics of drug response.

The same dose of the same drug produces different outcomes in different people, and a significant fraction of that variation is explained by inherited variation in the genes coding for drug-metabolizing enzymes, drug transporters, and drug targets.

Genetics is one variable among many — age, weight, kidney function, other drugs, adherence — but for the drugs we care about most, it is the variable that, once known, changes what you should prescribe.
Two.
The cytochrome P450 enzymes — the liver's metabolic kitchen — handle about 75% of all prescription drugs.
The five names to remember are CYP3A4, CYP2D6, CYP2C9, CYP2C19, and CYP2B6.
Each has known variants, organized by the star allele system, that translate into one of five metabolizer phenotypes: poor, intermediate, normal, rapid, and ultrarapid.

The poor metabolizer is the slow chef; the ultrarapid metabolizer is the fast chef; both are dangerous in opposite directions, and which direction depends on whether the drug is active in its parent form or is a prodrug needing activation.
Three.

The canonical drug-gene pairs every clinician should know are: warfarin with CYP2C9 and VKORC1; clopidogrel with CYP2C19; codeine and tramadol with CYP2D6; abacavir with HLA-B\5701; carbamazepine with HLA-B\1502; thiopurines (6-MP, azathioprine) with TPMT and NUDT15; 5-FU with DPYD; irinotecan with UGT1A1.
For some of these, testing is now mandatory before prescribing.
For all of them, testing changes patient outcomes.
Four.

The pharmacogenomics infrastructure — PharmGKB for the evidence base, CPIC for the clinical guidelines, preemptive panel testing for the workflow — has matured to the point where any prescriber willing to follow the guidelines can practice pharmacogenomics effectively.
The bottleneck is no longer the science.

It's the integration of genotype data into clinical workflows, the EHR systems that have to deliver the right information at the right moment, the reimbursement that has to make testing accessible, and the training that has to bring practicing clinicians up to current standards.
Five.
Pharmacogenomics has an ancestry problem.
Most of the field's evidence base was developed in European populations, and allele frequencies and clinical guidelines may not transfer cleanly to other groups.
The field is catching up, but slowly, and in the meantime non-European patients are systematically less well-served by default panels and default guidelines.
The solution is to use comprehensive panels that include population-relevant variants, and to be aware of population-specific guidance where it exists.
Six.
The future is preemptive, universal, and invisible.
Genotype every patient once.
Store the results.
Check automatically with every prescription.
Alert the prescriber only when action is needed.
Let pharmacogenomics become as routine, and as taken-for-granted, as checking a patient's allergies or their kidney function before writing the script.
We're not there yet, but the trajectory is clear, and the destination is one where adverse drug reactions of the preventable kind become rare events.
The last thing I want to say is this.
Pharmacogenomics is one of the rare areas of medicine where what we learn from genetics translates almost immediately into action that helps a specific patient in front of you.
It's not a research promise.
It's not a maybe-someday breakthrough.
For abacavir, for thiopurines, for 5-FU, for clopidogrel, for codeine in children, the genetic test before the prescription is what stands between a fine outcome and a catastrophic one.
People are alive today because their CYP genotype was checked before the chemotherapy infusion.
People are alive today because their HLA-B\*5701 status was tested before they were started on abacavir.
Pharmacogenomics is, in this sense, one of the cleanest cases of precision medicine actually delivering on its promise.
Not for every drug.
Not for every patient.
But for a growing list of drug-gene pairs, the answer is unambiguous: test before you prescribe, and you will help more people and harm fewer.
The rest is implementation.
That's the field.
Thanks for sticking with me for two hours.
Thanks, everyone.
Take care of yourselves.
Good work today.
